Numerical Linear Algebra

From QR to Eigenvalues: Full QR, Diagonalization, and the Road to SVD

From QR to Eigenvalues: Full QR, Diagonalization, and the Road to SVD

The last few chapters built the QR factorization as a tool for solving least-squares problems stably. This chapter does two things. First, it fills in a detail we glossed over: QR actually comes in two flavors — “thin” and “full” — and understanding the difference reveals something structural about the four fundamental subspaces of AAA. Second, it pivots toward an entirely new question: instead of factoring AAA to solve Ax=bAx=bAx=b, can we factor AAA to understand how it acts as a transformation — stretching space along certain special directions? That question leads to eigenvalues, diagonalization, and ultimately the singular value decomposition (SVD), which generalizes eigen-decomposition to matrices that aren’t even square.

Full QR Factorization

Every factorization we’ve built so far — reflectors chained together, Givens rotations chained together — produced a square orthogonal matrix QQQ, even when AAA itself was rectangular. For a tall, skinny matrix A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n with m>nm > nm>n, this means Q∈Rm×mQ \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times m}Q∈Rm×m has more columns than AAA does. What are those extra columns for?

Full QR Factorization

For A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n (tall and skinny, m>nm > nm>n): A=QR,Q∈Rm×m orthogonal,R∈Rm×n upper triangular (with an all-zero bottom block).A = QR, \qquad Q \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times m} \text{ orthogonal}, \qquad R \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n} \text{ upper triangular (with an all-zero bottom block)}.A=QR,Q∈Rm×m orthogonal,R∈Rm×n upper triangular (with an all-zero bottom block).

Because RRR‘s bottom m−nm-nm−n rows are entirely zero, we can split both QQQ and RRR into blocks and discard the part of the product that multiplies against zero:

A=QR=[Q^Q~][R^0]=Q^R^+Q~⋅0=Q^R^.A = QR = \begin{bmatrix}\hat Q & \tilde Q\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\hat R \\ 0\end{bmatrix} = \hat Q\hat R + \tilde Q\cdot 0 = \hat Q\hat R.A=QR=[Q^​​Q~​​][R^0​]=Q^​R^+Q~​⋅0=Q^​R^.

Here Q^∈Rm×n\hat Q \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}Q^​∈Rm×n consists of the first nnn columns of QQQ, and R^∈Rn×n\hat R\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}R^∈Rn×n is the nonzero top block of RRR — square and upper triangular. This smaller factorization, A=Q^R^A = \hat Q\hat RA=Q^​R^, is exactly the thin QR factorization used throughout the least-squares chapter: it’s all you need to solve min⁡x∥Ax−b∥2\min_x\|Ax-b\|_2minx​∥Ax−b∥2​, since the extra columns Q~\tilde QQ~​ never interacted with RRR in the first place.

So the “full” factorization isn’t a different answer — it’s the same Q^R^\hat Q\hat RQ^​R^ padded out with extra orthogonal columns Q~\tilde QQ~​ so that QQQ is square. Those extra columns turn out to have a precise geometric meaning.

What the extra columns represent
  • The columns of Q^\hat QQ^​ form an orthonormal basis for Col⁡(A)\operatorname{Col}(A)Col(A) (also called Range⁡(A)\operatorname{Range}(A)Range(A)) — the same column space that least squares projects bbb onto.
  • The remaining columns qjq_jqj​ for j>nj > nj>n are orthogonal to every column of Q^\hat QQ^​, and therefore orthogonal to all of Range⁡(A)\operatorname{Range}(A)Range(A). Assuming AAA has full column rank, these columns form a basis for Nul⁡(AT)\operatorname{Nul}(A^T)Nul(AT) — the left null space of AAA.

This is a nice structural payoff: the single matrix QQQ, built purely from reflectors or rotators with no reference to null spaces at all, ends up encoding both fundamental subspaces of AAA (its column space and its left null space) as two orthogonal blocks. This is a preview of a theme that will return with full force in the SVD at the end of this chapter: factorizations built from orthogonal matrices tend to reveal a matrix’s underlying subspace structure “for free.”

Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

QR factorization answers “how do I solve Ax=bAx=bAx=b stably?” A completely different question is: does AAA, acting as a linear transformation, have any special directions that it simply stretches rather than rotates or distorts? That’s the question eigenvectors answer.

Eigenvalue / Eigenvector

A nonzero vector x⃗\vec xx is an eigenvector of AAA, with corresponding eigenvalue λ\lambdaλ, if Ax⃗=λx⃗.A\vec x = \lambda \vec x.Ax=λx. In words: multiplying x⃗\vec xx by AAA doesn’t change its direction — it only scales it by the factor λ\lambdaλ.

Uniqueness (and non-uniqueness)

For a fixed eigenvector x⃗\vec xx, the eigenvalue λ\lambdaλ it produces is unique — there’s only one scaling factor associated with that direction. Eigenvectors, however, are not unique: any nonzero scalar multiple of x⃗\vec xx is also a valid eigenvector with the same eigenvalue λ\lambdaλ, and more generally the set of all eigenvectors sharing a given λ\lambdaλ (together with the zero vector) forms a subspace called the eigenspace of λ\lambdaλ.

Eigenvalue Decomposition

If a matrix has enough eigenvectors, we can package all of them into a single factorization that describes AAA‘s action completely.

Eigenvalue Decomposition

Collect the eigenvectors as columns of a matrix, and the eigenvalues as a diagonal matrix:

X=[x⃗1x⃗2⋯x⃗n],Λ=[λ1λ2⋱λn].X = \begin{bmatrix}\vec x_1 & \vec x_2 & \cdots & \vec x_n\end{bmatrix}, \qquad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix}\lambda_1 & & & \\ & \lambda_2 & & \\ & & \ddots & \\ & & & \lambda_n\end{bmatrix}.X=[x1​​x2​​⋯​xn​​],Λ=​λ1​​λ2​​⋱​λn​​​.

If AAA has nnn linearly independent eigenvectors (equivalently, the number of independent eigenvalue directions equals rank⁡(A)=n\operatorname{rank}(A) = nrank(A)=n), then AX=XΛ⟺A=XΛX−1,AX = X\Lambda \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad A = X\Lambda X^{-1},AX=XΛ⟺A=XΛX−1, and we say AAA is diagonalizable — or equivalently, that AAA is similar to the diagonal matrix Λ\LambdaΛ.

Why is AX=XΛAX=X\LambdaAX=XΛ equivalent to stacking up the eigenvector equations? Multiplying AAA by the matrix XXX column-by-column just applies Ax⃗j=λjx⃗jA\vec x_j = \lambda_j\vec x_jAxj​=λj​xj​ to each column simultaneously — so AX=XΛAX = X\LambdaAX=XΛ is nothing more than a compact way of writing “all nnn eigenvector equations at once.”

Similar matrices

Two matrices A,BA, BA,B are similar if there exists an invertible matrix MMM such that A=MBM−1.A = MBM^{-1}.A=MBM−1. Similar matrices represent the same linear transformation, just expressed in different coordinate systems (bases) — MMM is the change-of-basis matrix. Diagonalizability says precisely that AAA‘s transformation looks like simple axis-scaling once you view it in the coordinate system given by its eigenvectors.

Not every matrix is diagonalizable

The decomposition A=XΛX−1A = X\Lambda X^{-1}A=XΛX−1 requires XXX to be invertible, which requires nnn linearly independent eigenvectors. Some matrices simply don’t have enough — repeated eigenvalues can come with an eigenspace that’s “too small,” in which case no such XXX exists and AAA is not diagonalizable at all.

A well-behaved special case: symmetric positive definite matrices

For one important class of matrices, diagonalizability is guaranteed, and even better, the eigenvector matrix can be chosen to be orthogonal — directly reconnecting this section to the reflectors and rotators built earlier in the course.

Spectral decomposition of SPD matrices

If AAA is symmetric positive definite (SPD), its eigenvectors can be chosen orthonormal, and the decomposition can be written as a sum of rank-one pieces: A=∑i=1nλi qiqiT(a sum of rank-one matrices)A = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\, q_iq_i^T \qquad \text{(a sum of rank-one matrices)}A=∑i=1n​λi​qi​qiT​(a sum of rank-one matrices) where {qi}\{q_i\}{qi​} is an orthonormal eigenvector basis. Each term λiqiqiT\lambda_i q_iq_i^Tλi​qi​qiT​ should look familiar: it’s exactly the projector Pqi=qiqiTP_{q_i} = q_iq_i^TPqi​​=qi​qiT​ from the very first chapter of this course, scaled by λi\lambda_iλi​. So an SPD matrix is, structurally, nothing more than a weighted sum of orthogonal projections onto its eigenvector directions.

Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

Eigenvalue decomposition has a hard requirement built into its definition: AAA must be square, since Ax⃗A\vec xAx and λx⃗\lambda\vec xλx have to live in the same space for the equation Ax⃗=λx⃗A\vec x=\lambda\vec xAx=λx to even make sense. But most of the matrices in this course — the tall, skinny A∈Rm×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n from least squares and full QR — are not square at all. The singular value decomposition generalizes the same underlying idea (find special directions that AAA acts on simply) to matrices of any shape.

Singular Value Decomposition

For any A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n (no requirement that m=nm=nm=n): A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT where U∈Rm×mU\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}U∈Rm×m and V∈Rn×nV\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}V∈Rn×n are orthogonal, and Σ∈Rm×n\Sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}Σ∈Rm×n is diagonal (in the generalized, rectangular sense):

Σ=[σ1σ2⋱σr0],\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix}\sigma_1 & & & \\ & \sigma_2 & & \\ & & \ddots & \\ & & & \sigma_r \\ & & & & 0\end{bmatrix},Σ=​σ1​​σ2​​⋱​σr​​0​​,

padded with zero rows or columns as needed to match the shape of AAA.

The relationship between SVD and eigen-decomposition is direct rather than coincidental: the columns of VVV are eigenvectors of ATAA^TAATA, the columns of UUU are eigenvectors of AATAA^TAAT, and the singular values satisfy σi=λi(ATA)\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(A^TA)}σi​=λi​(ATA)​. In other words, SVD is what you get by applying eigen-decomposition to the square, symmetric matrices ATAA^TAATA and AATAA^TAAT built from AAA — which brings the discussion full circle back to the SPD spectral decomposition above, since ATAA^TAATA is always symmetric positive semi-definite.

Why this matters going forward

Recall from the least-squares chapters that forming ATAA^TAATA is exactly the numerically dangerous step we worked hard to avoid, because it squares the condition number. SVD is the tool that lets us understand and work with ATAA^TAATA‘s eigenstructure without ever forming that unstable product directly — which is precisely why SVD was flagged earlier as the most numerically robust (if most expensive) way to solve least-squares problems, and why it will be the natural next topic to develop in full.
